fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund is a perfect starting point for your aggressive plan. It mixes large companies with solid track records and mid-sized companies that are growing fast. It's managed by experienced funds at ICICI Prudential, has a huge pool of money (over 7,800 crores), and a consistent 20% 3-year return. Since it's held for >1 year, only gains above ₹1.25L in a year will be taxed at 12.5% (much better than the 30% income tax rate).

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: You asked for exposure to mid-caps, and this is one of the best builders. It intentionally skips the huge, slow-growth big companies and focuses on the 'have-not-yet' the ones that jump 5x or 10x in quality lifecycles. It's run by Motilal Oswal, who are heavyweights in mid-cap picking. Consistent 21% 3-year 3yr 3-year return. It's a bit less consistent than the ICICI fund above but has much higher upside. If you're aggressive, this is your ticket to high growth. Because it has more fluctuation, it's best suited for your 20-year horizon where short-term drops matter less.

fund.name: HDFC Mid Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Another proven mid-cap fund. HDFC has been around for decades, and this fund has consistently outperformed peers. It has a 24,000 crore AUM which stabilzes it, but it also delivers amazing 25% 3-year returns. It's a good combination of stability and growth. Like the ICICI fund, it's equity-taxed so you get the long-term capital gains benefit of 12.5% for gains above ₹1.25L.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This fund focuses specifically on mid-cap companies with a 30-year track record. Nippon Life is one of the oldest and most reliable fund houses. It has powerful momentum and has delivered 26% returns over 3 years. It's a pure-play mid-cap fund so it will be the most volatile piece of your portfolio, but with 30+ years of expertise, it's managed by veterans who know how to navigate downturns.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Small-caps are the highest risk but also the highest reward. Think of them as betting on the next big unicorn. This fund has a 15-year track record and has delivered 21% 5-year returns. It's a 31% 5-year return. It's the only way to get exposure to the smallest, most volatile stocks in hopes of massive gains. Since it's equity-taxed, long-term gains are tax-efficient. But be warned: this fund can drop 40-50% in a bad year.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund is unique. It doesn't stick to one category. It picks the best opportunities anywhere — sometimes large, sometimes small, sometimes international (US). It's run by a team that's very disciplined. It has huge AUM (88k crores) which gives stability. It's only 7% 5-year returns, but it has a great safety margin (low beta) and has protected investors during downturns. It's a good 'ballast' for your aggressive portfolio.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund focuses on the biggest, most stable companies — the ones that drive India's economy. It has a 15-year track record and has delivered 18% 3-year returns. It's run by a top-tier team. It's the safest bet in your equity portion. It's the only one in the portfolio with a beta close to 1, meaning it moves with the market. It's a great base for your plan.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Multicap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund is a bit more aggressive than the Large Cap fund, with a bit more exposure to mid and small caps. It has a long 31-year track record and has delivered 19% 3-year returns. It's a good way to get a bit more growth without picking individual funds. It's also equity-taxed.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund simply buys the top 150 mid-cap companies, so there's no manager risk. It has a very low fee (0.2% vs 1%+ for active funds). It has been a great performer with 18% 5-year returns. It's a good way to get a cheap, broad exposure to mid-caps. It's currently in a good spot.

Gold is your 'insurance' policy. It's not about making money; it's about losing less when everything else crashes (like in inflation or wars). It's run by UTI, a very large and stable fund house. It has delivered 25% 3-year returns. It's slightly slab-taxed, so gains are taxed at 30%, but it's still useful for your aggressive plan. It's a good hedge against the volatility in your equity portion.

fund.name: Franklin India SHORT TERM INCOME PLAN - Direct - GROWTH

allocations.rationale: This fund is one of the best short-duration debt funds, with high interest rates currently. It has a 24-year track record and has delivered 31% 5-year returns. It's a 'slab-taxed' fund, so returns are taxed at 30%, but it's still useful for a bit of safety. It's a great way to park some money in a low-risk option.

Using your existing corpus of 2.5M (after loans), we're allocating ₹1M (40%) to gold via a lumpsum. This is your 'insurance money' — it's safe and will protect you if the stock market crashes. Gold has historically been a good hedge. It's run by ICICI Prudential, one of the most reliable fund houses. It's a slight slab-taxed fund, so gains are taxed at 30%, but it's still useful.

fund.name: UTI Long Term Advantage Fund Series V - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This is an ELSS fund (tax-saving) for you. It gives you 80C deduction up to ₹1.5L on investment and has a 3-year lock-in. It's equity-taxed, so long-term gains are only taxed at 12.5% (LGC). It's a good way to save tax and invest in equity. It's run by UTI, a top-tier fund house. It has a 9-year track record and has delivered 19% 3-year returns. It's a great way to get equity exposure with tax benefits.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a 'Direct Mutual Fund' account on an app like Kuvera, Groww, or Coin (these apps have all these funds and direct plans). Step 2: Transfer your monthly surplus of ₹3,00,000 to the linked bank account. Step 3: Start SIPs in all these funds on the 10th or 15th of every month. Step 4: For the existing 2.5M corpus: keep ₹1M in the UTI Gold ETF (lumpsum), keep ₹1M in the Franklin Short Term Income Plan (lumpsum), and use the remaining 500k to start additional SIPs in the growth funds. Step 5: Keep 500k as an emergency fund (liquidity) — don't invest it yet. Step 6: Review your portfolio every 6 months. Step 7: When you buy your luxury apartment in 3 years, use the lowest-risk funds (gold, debt) to fund 50% of it, and sell some equity for the rest if needed. For financial independence in 10–15 years, keep riding the waves until you're ready to retire.
review_checkpoints: 3 months: Check if all SIPs are running correctly. If the SIP failed once, fix it. [No action needed]. Step 6: If any fund's returns fall significantly behind the category average, consider switching to a better performer (but only after 1 year).

1 year: Check for tax-loss harvesting (if any fund is down, sell it to claim loss). Check if you need to sell 1M of equity for the apartment. If needed, sell from the higher-risk funds (Mid Cap, Small Cap) first.
Every 6 months: Rebalance if any single fund exceeds 20% of your portfolio (e.g., if Small Cap grows to 25%, trim it and move to cash/debt).
When buying the apartment in 3 years: Switch out of Mid/Small Cap funds into Gold/Debt for the 3 years before purchase to avoid short-term capital gains tax.
Age 35: If your portfolio drops 30% (common in mid-cap/small-cap crashes), do not sell. Instead, increase SIPs by 20% if you have cash.
Age 45 (Financial Independence): Check if your corpus has reached 1Cr. If not, consider shifting to lower-risk funds (Large Cap, Debt) in the last 3 years.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year (e.g., every March), check your portfolio's total value. If any fund has grown too large (e.g., from 5% to 15%), sell the excess and put it into smaller funds to keep your original mix. This 'rebalancing' ensures you don't become too aggressive or too conservative. For example, if Small Cap (riskiest) grows from 10% to 20%, sell the extra ₹10k and move it to Gold. This keeps you in your plan.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity allocation is overwhelmingly active. Only one fund (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund) is a passive/index fund, representing 5% of the total equity allocation (5% out of 90% equity = ~5.5% of equity sleeve). All others are actively managed large-cap, mid-cap, small-cap, or flexi-cap funds. Hence, the passive fraction in equity is very low, scoring ~0.1.

turnover_score: The plan recommends annual rebalancing ('once a year') and semi-annual review with rebalancing if any fund exceeds 20%. While not extremely frequent, this is more active than pure buy-and-hold. Tactical shifts are implied (e.g., shifting to debt/gold before apartment purchase). This suggests moderate turnover, scoring ~0.3.

cost_emphasis_score: Costs (expense ratios, fees) are rarely mentioned. The only cost reference is noting the low fee of the Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (0.2%), but this is not a central theme. Fund selection is driven by performance, track record, and fund house reputation, not cost minimisation. Hence, cost is not a primary criterion, scoring ~0.2.

research_vs_cost_score: The rationale focuses heavily on fund manager expertise, fund house reputation, track records, and performance (e.g., 'Motilal Oswal, who are heavyweights in mid-cap picking', 'run by veterans'). There is no emphasis on broad market exposure or index-based philosophy. This indicates deep reliance on active management and research, scoring ~0.1.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for long-term goals (financial independence in 10-15 years, 20-year horizon), with advice to stay invested through volatility. However, it includes medium-term tactical shifts (e.g., asset shift 3 years before apartment purchase), which slightly reduces the long-term purity. Still, the core orientation is long-term, scoring ~0.7.

portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds are actively managed with rationales based on manager skill, stock selection, and outperformance. Even the one index fund is a small portion. Risk metrics are missing, but category and rationale confirm active mandates. Debt and gold allocations are active funds (Franklin, UTI Gold ETF), though gold ETFs are inherently passive-like; however, multiple gold funds suggest no passive intent. Overall, the portfolio is clearly actively managed, scoring ~0.15.

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows strong goal alignment by focusing on aggressive growth funds (mid-cap, small-cap, flexi-cap) suited to the investor's 20-year horizon and goal of building a 5Cr corpus and achieving financial independence. Diversification is excellent across fund houses, categories (large, mid, small, flexi, index, gold), and styles (active and passive), reducing concentration risk. Risk-return is appropriate for an aggressive investor, though the high allocation to mid/small caps (40% of equity) introduces significant volatility, which is acknowledged but not fully mitigated in the short-term goal (luxury apartment in 3 years). Internal consistency is moderate: while the equity-heavy strategy is coherent, there's a contradiction in the setup phase—recommending to park ₹1M in Franklin Short Term Income Plan (debt) as lumpsum, which is slab-taxed at 30%, while ignoring tax-efficient arbitrage or aggressive hybrid funds. This flaw is amplified in tax_efficiency scoring: the investor is in the 30% tax slab, yet 10% of the portfolio (Franklin debt fund + gold ETFs) is exposed to slab taxation. Gold ETFs are correctly identified as slightly taxed, but the rationale fails to explore equity-taxed alternatives for debt exposure. ELSS inclusion (10%) is appropriate for 80C savings under the old regime, but the plan does not justify why more 80C headroom isn't used (up to ₹1.5L/year). However, the biggest tax inefficiency is the use of a slab-taxed short-term debt fund for a long-term investor in the highest tax bracket—arbitrage or aggressive hybrid funds would offer similar risk with equity taxation. The plan mentions tax treatment but fails to optimize it, hence a neutral tax_efficiency score of 0.5.
